cell_specs.implementation_plan: Setup cell. Import numpy as np, matplotlib.pyplot as plt, and 'from ipywidgets import interact, FloatSlider, IntSlider, FloatLogSlider' guarded by try/except. Attempt inline plotting with get_ipython().run_line_magic('matplotlib', 'inline') wrapped in try/except so the cell still runs outside IPython. Set module-level SEED = 42 and create a seeded generator rng = np.random.default_rng(SEED). Configure default figure styling: plt.rcParams['figure.figsize'] = (8, 4.5) and plt.rcParams['figure.dpi'] = 110. Define a helper style_axes(ax, title=None, xlabel='x', ylabel='y') that sets labels, optional title, and grid(alpha=0.3). Print a one-line confirmation showing numpy/matplotlib versions and the seed. Expose np, plt, rng, SEED, style_axes, WIDGETS_AVAILABLE and the slider/interact names module-wide.
state_variables: np, plt, interact, FloatSlider, IntSlider, FloatLogSlider (module imports)
error_handling: Wrap the matplotlib inline magic in try/except so the cell runs outside IPython.
Wrap 'from ipywidgets import ...' in try/except ImportError; on failure set WIDGETS_AVAILABLE=False and print install hint '!pip install ipywidgets' so later cells fall back to static rendering.
test_checks: assert 'np' in dir() and 'plt' in dir()
assert isinstance(rng, np.random.Generator)
Confirmation print executes without raising.

cell_specs.implementation_plan: Generate the synthetic linear dataset reused by C06 and C08. Set true_w = 2.0, true_b = -1.0, N = 40, noise_std = 1.0. Draw x_train = np.sort(rng.uniform(-3, 3, size=N)) for a clean left-to-right plot. Compute y_train = true_w * x_train + true_b + rng.normal(0, noise_std, size=N). Create one figure: scatter (x_train, y_train) and overlay a single example candidate line with guess parameters (w_guess=1.0, b_guess=0.0) over a dense grid xs = np.linspace(x_train.min(), x_train.max(), 100). Use style_axes for labels and a legend distinguishing data vs candidate line. plt.show(). Keep arrays at module scope.
error_handling: Assert x_train and y_train have equal length before plotting.
Use rng (not the np.random global) so re-running after C02 reproduces the same data.
test_checks: assert x_train.shape == y_train.shape == (N,)
assert np.all(np.diff(x_train) >= 0)  # sorted
Figure displays a scatter plus one candidate line.

cell_specs.implementation_plan: Define loss(w, b) returning MSE over x_train, y_train: preds = w*x_train + b; return float(np.mean((preds - y_train)**2)). Build an interactive explorer plot_line(w, b) that computes preds on a dense grid, plots data scatter + candidate line, sets title to f'Loss L(w,b) = {loss(w,b):.3f}', uses style_axes and fixed y-limits (computed once from the data range, padded) so the view does not jump while sliders move. Wire with interact(plot_line, w=FloatSlider(min=-1, max=5, step=0.1, value=1.0), b=FloatSlider(min=-5, max=5, step=0.1, value=0.0)). If WIDGETS_AVAILABLE is False, call plot_line(true_w, true_b) once for a static fallback.
state_variables: loss (function, reused by C08 and C09)
Fixed axis limits derived from x_train/y_train for a stable interactive view
error_handling: loss casts the result to float to avoid numpy scalar display quirks in the title.
Guard interact behind WIDGETS_AVAILABLE; provide a static call fallback so the cell never errors when ipywidgets is missing.
Fix y-axis limits so rapid slider changes do not cause flickering rescale.
test_checks: assert loss(true_w, true_b) < loss(0.0, 0.0)  # truth fits better than the zero line
assert loss(true_w, true_b) >= 0.0
plot_line(1.0, 0.0) runs without error.

cell_specs.implementation_plan: Implement gradient_descent(lr, n_steps, w0=0.0, b0=0.0). Use analytic MSE gradients: with preds=w*x_train+b, err=preds-y_train, dL/dw = (2/N)*np.sum(err*x_train), dL/db = (2/N)*np.sum(err). Loop n_steps times updating w,b and appending (w, b, loss(w,b)) to a trajectory each iteration (include the initial point). Return trajectory as a dict of numpy arrays {'w':..., 'b':..., 'loss':...}. After defining, run one example (lr=0.05, n_steps=50) and print final (w,b) and final loss alongside (true_w, true_b) and loss(true_w,true_b) for comparison.
function_signatures: gradient_descent(lr: float, n_steps: int, w0: float = 0.0, b0: float = 0.0) -> dict
state_variables: N reused from C04 for gradient normalization
error_handling: Cast n_steps to int and guard n_steps >= 1.
Detect numeric blow-up: if loss is not finite, break early and still return the partial trajectory so C09 can visualize divergence without raising.
Use float arrays in the trajectory to keep matplotlib happy.
test_checks: traj = gradient_descent(0.05, 200); assert traj['loss'][-1] < traj['loss'][0]
assert abs(traj['w'][-1]-true_w) < 0.5 and abs(traj['b'][-1]-true_b) < 0.5  # converges near truth
assert len(traj['w']) == len(traj['loss'])

cell_specs.implementation_plan: Interactive GD visualizer. Precompute a loss surface grid once at module scope: W = np.linspace(-1,5,80), B = np.linspace(-5,5,80), WW,BB = np.meshgrid(W,B), and ZZ where ZZ[i,j] = loss(WW[i,j], BB[i,j]) (vectorize or small loop over the grid). Define show_descent(lr, n_steps): call gradient_descent(lr, n_steps), then make a 1x2 figure: (left) contourf/contour of ZZ over (W,B) with the trajectory path drawn as line+markers and the true (true_w,true_b) marked with a star; (right) loss-vs-iteration curve (semilogy when all finite and positive). Title reflects final loss or 'diverged' if non-finite. Wire interact(show_descent, lr=FloatLogSlider(base=10, min=-3, max=0.5, step=0.1, value=0.05), n_steps=IntSlider(min=5, max=300, step=5, value=50)). Static fallback calls show_descent(0.05, 50) if widgets unavailable.
state_variables: W, B, WW, BB, ZZ (precomputed loss-surface grid arrays, computed once)
error_handling: If the trajectory contains non-finite values, clip/skip them when plotting the path and label the figure 'diverged (try a smaller learning rate)'.
Right plot: only use semilogy when all loss values are finite and > 0; otherwise use a linear plot to avoid log errors.
Compute ZZ once outside show_descent to keep slider updates responsive.
Guard interact behind WIDGETS_AVAILABLE with a static fallback call.
test_checks: assert ZZ.shape == WW.shape == BB.shape
show_descent(0.05, 50) runs without error (convergent case)
show_descent(1.5, 50) runs without error and is labeled as diverging (large lr)

cell_specs.implementation_plan: Create a nonlinear dataset for the overfitting demo and split into train/test. Define a true function g(x)=np.sin(1.5*x). Sample M=30 points x_all = np.sort(rng.uniform(-3,3,size=M)); y_all = g(x_all) + rng.normal(0, 0.25, size=M). Split via a shuffled index: idx = rng.permutation(M); cut at ~60% train; produce x_train2,y_train2,x_test2,y_test2, each re-sorted by x for clean plotting. Scatter train vs test points in different colors/markers and overlay the true function curve (light dashed) over a dense grid. Use style_axes + legend. Keep arrays at module scope.
function_signatures: g(x: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray  # true nonlinear function
error_handling: Assert train and test sets are non-empty and disjoint by index.
Re-sort each split by x before plotting/overlay to avoid zig-zag lines.
Use rng for reproducibility.
test_checks: assert len(x_train2) + len(x_test2) == M
assert len(x_train2) > 0 and len(x_test2) > 0
Figure shows distinguishable train vs test points.

cell_specs.implementation_plan: Interactive polynomial overfitting demo. Define a helper mse(y_true, y_pred)=float(np.mean((y_true-y_pred)**2)). plot_poly(degree): fit coeffs = np.polyfit(x_train2, y_train2, degree), evaluate train MSE and test MSE via np.polyval, plot a dense smooth fitted curve over the padded x-range, scatter train and test points, set title f'degree={degree}  train MSE={tr:.3f}  test MSE={te:.3f}', and clamp y-limits to a sensible range based on data so high-degree blow-ups do not destroy the view. interact(plot_poly, degree=IntSlider(min=1, max=15, step=1, value=1)). Static fallback calls plot_poly(9).
state_variables: Fixed y-limits derived from the data range to bound high-degree curves
error_handling: Suppress np.polyfit RankWarning via warnings and cap degree at len(x_train2)-1 if degree is too large; wrap polyfit in try/except np.linalg.LinAlgError.
Clamp plot y-limits so an exploding high-degree fit does not make the figure unreadable.
Cast degree to int.
test_checks: tr1 = mse(y_train2, np.polyval(np.polyfit(x_train2,y_train2,1), x_train2)); tr9 = mse(y_train2, np.polyval(np.polyfit(x_train2,y_train2,9), x_train2)); assert tr9 <= tr1 + 1e-9  # higher degree lowers train error
plot_poly(1) and plot_poly(12) run without error
Train MSE and test MSE are both shown in the title.

cell_specs.implementation_plan: Regularization (ridge) remedy demo at a fixed high degree DEG=12. Build polynomial design matrices via np.vander(x, DEG+1). Implement ridge_fit(X, y, lam): theta = np.linalg.solve(X.T@X + lam*I, X.T@y) with I an identity whose intercept-column diagonal entry is set to 0 (do not penalize the bias). plot_ridge(log_lambda): lam = 10**log_lambda, fit on train, compute train/test MSE (reusing mse from C12), plot the fitted curve + train/test scatter with bounded y-limits, title shows lambda and both errors. interact(plot_ridge, log_lambda=FloatSlider(min=-6, max=3, step=0.5, value=-3)) with lam derived as 10**log_lambda. Static fallback calls plot_ridge(-3).
function_signatures: ridge_fit(X: np.ndarray, y: np.ndarray, lam: float) -> np.ndarray  # returns theta
state_variables: Design matrix via np.vander; identity mask excluding the intercept from the penalty
Reuses mse from C12 and the C11 datasets
error_handling: Use np.linalg.solve on the regularized normal equations; if singular (np.linalg.LinAlgError), fall back to np.linalg.lstsq.
Do not penalize the intercept column (zero that diagonal entry) to avoid bias-shrinkage artifacts.
Clamp plot y-limits; compute lam = 10**log_lambda safely.
test_checks: X = np.vander(x_train2, DEG+1); t_small = ridge_fit(X, y_train2, 1e-6); t_big = ridge_fit(X, y_train2, 1e3); assert np.linalg.norm(t_big) < np.linalg.norm(t_small)  # larger lambda shrinks coefficients
plot_ridge(-6) and plot_ridge(2) run without error
Increasing lambda visibly smooths the fitted curve.

cross_cell_invariants: A single seeded RNG (rng, SEED=42 from C02) is the only randomness source; all data-generating cells (C04, C11) draw from it so results are reproducible top-to-bottom.
x_train, y_train, true_w, true_b, N are created exactly once in C04 and reused unchanged by C06, C08, and C09.
x_train2, y_train2, x_test2, y_test2 are created exactly once in C11 and reused unchanged by C12 and C13.
loss(w, b) is defined once in C06 and reused by C08 (validation/printout) and C09 (surface + curve).
gradient_descent is defined in C08 and reused by C09; mse is defined in C12 and reused by C13.
Every code cell depends only on earlier cells; the notebook must execute strictly top-to-bottom with no forward references.
WIDGETS_AVAILABLE from C02 gates every interact() call; each interactive cell (C06, C09, C12, C13) has a static fallback so the notebook never errors when ipywidgets is missing.
All interactive plotting callbacks use fixed/bounded axis limits so slider changes do not cause distracting rescaling or unreadable blow-ups.
assumptions: numpy, matplotlib, and ipywidgets are importable in the runtime (Colab/Jupyter); a guarded fallback handles missing ipywidgets.
np.random.default_rng is available (NumPy >= 1.17) and is used instead of legacy np.random for reproducibility.
Polynomial fitting uses np.polyfit/np.vander closed-form least squares (and closed-form ridge), not gradient descent, for speed and numerical stability, matching the Stage 2 assumption.

The true linear parameters (true_w=2.0, true_b=-1.0) and nonlinear function g(x)=sin(1.5x) are illustrative; exact constants may be tuned by demo-coder as long as the invariants and test checks above still hold.
Interactive cells rely on ipywidgets.interact; '%matplotlib inline' (or the Colab default) provides static-image rendering of each interaction frame.
